coredata.dc:title: Ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iron ion and ascorbic acid
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Zengchun
Sun, Xiaofeng
Jiao, Jianmei
Xin, Xia
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a convenient on-off fluorescent probe was developed to detect iron ion (Fe3+) with sensitivity and selectivity by using highly luminescent ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which was prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment using citric acid and 1-Aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium. The sensitivity of CQDs for Fe3+ was examined by measuring the fluorescence (FL) intensity of various concentrations of Fe3+ in CQDs solution and the selectivity of CQDs for Fe3+ was examined by adding different metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Ba2+) into CQDs solution, respectively. The results indicated that the FL intensity of CQDs showed a good linear response to the concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–300μmolL−1 (R2 =0.99) with a detection limit of 13.68μmolL−1. Furthermore, based on the redox mechanism, after the fluorescent of CQDs was quenched by Fe3+, different neurotransmitters and amino acids were added and the FL test show that only ascorbic acid (AA) can recover the intensity of the CQDs/Fe3+, indicating that CQDs/Fe3+ can be further used as a on-off fluorescent probe for the detection of AA. It is expected that our strategy may offer a new approach for developing rapid, low cost and sensitive CQDs-based sensors for environmetal and biological sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Ionic liquid
On-off probe
Iron ion
Ascorbic acid

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ion

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of CQDs quenched by Fe3+ ion and recovered by AA.
(A) HR-TEM of CQDs. Inset: the lattice structure of the CQDs. (B) The corresponding size distribution histogram of CQDs.
Emission spectra of CQDs with increasing λex from 340nm on the left with 20nm increments. Inset: photographs of CQDs solutions taken under daylight (left) and 365nm UV (right).

Fluorescence spectra of CQDs (4×10−4 mgmL−1). (A) Upon the addition of 10mmolL−1 various metal nitrate (Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, Ca2+, K+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Ba2+). (B) Upon the addition of 10mmolL−1 Fe3+ +10mmolL−1 other metal nitrate. (C) Selectivity of CQDs toward Fe3+: Column charts of I/I 0 of CQDs+metal ions at 450nm. The red bars represent the addition of various metal ions to the blank solution and the black bars represent the subsequent addition of Fe3+ (10mmolL−1) to the above solutions (CQDs+Mn+ +Fe3+). Excitation wavelength=380nm. (D) Photographs of the CQDs solution containing 10mmolL−1 various metal ions under 365nm UV light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs (4×10−4 mgmL−1) upon the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. (B) The variation of the fluorescence intensity as a function of the concentration of Fe3+. Inset of (B): Photographs of the CQDs solution upon the addition of different amount of Fe3+ under 365nm UV light, the concentrations of Fe3+ from left to right are 0, 1, 5 and 10mmolL−1, respectively.
The FL intensity of 4×10−4 mgmL−1 CQDs in different real waters of Jinan.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs (4×10−4 mgmL−1)/Fe3+ (10mmolL−1) upon the addition of 10mmolL−1 different biomolecules. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs (4×10−4 mgmL−1) upon the addition of 10mmolL−1 Fe3+ and 10mmolL−1 Fe2+. Inset of (B): Photographs of the CQDs (4×10−4 mgmL−1) (left) CQDs (4×10−4 mgmL−1) + Fe3+ (10mmolL−1) (middle) and CQDs (4×10−4 mgmL−1) + Fe2+ (10mmolL−1) (rigth) under 365nm UV light. (C) Column charts of I/I 0 of CQDs+Fe3+ +biomolecule. The blank bars represent the addition of various biomolecules to the blank solution (CQDs+Fe3+) and the red bars represent the subsequent addition of AA (10mmolL−1) to the above solutions (CQDs+Fe3+ +biomolecule). (D) Photographs of the CQDs (4×10−4 mgmL−1)/Fe3+ (10mmolL−1) upon the addition of different biomolecules under 365nm UV light. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Time-resolved FL spectra of CQDs with an excitation at 360nm in the absence (black) and in the presence of Fe3+ (red), and Fe3+/AA (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
TEM image of the sample of 4×10−4 mgmL−1 CQDs/10mmolL−1 Fe3+.
Fluorescence lifetimes obtained with two-exponential fit of the fluorescence decay curves of the CQDs alone, CQD/Fe3+, and CQD/Fe3+/AA, respectively.

head.ce:title: Ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for sensitive and selective detection of iron ion and ascorbic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Technology Research Center for Colloidal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Technology Research Center for Colloidal Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, Gansu Province 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: National Engineering Technology Research Center for Colloidal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan 250100, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Technology Research Center for Colloidal Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of CQDs quenched by Fe3+ ion and recovered by AA.

Herein, a convenient on-off fluorescent probe was developed to detect iron ion (Fe3+) with sensitivity and selectivity by using highly luminescent ionic liquid-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which was prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment using citric acid and 1-Aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium. The sensitivity of CQDs for Fe3+ was examined by measuring the fluorescence (FL) intensity of various concentrations of Fe3+ in CQDs solution and the selectivity of CQDs for Fe3+ was examined by adding different metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Ba2+) into CQDs solution, respectively. The results indicated that the FL intensity of CQDs showed a good linear response to the concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–300μmolL−1 (R2 =0.99) with a detection limit of 13.68μmolL−1. Furthermore, based on the redox mechanism, after the fluorescent of CQDs was quenched by Fe3+, different neurotransmitters and amino acids were added and the FL test show that only ascorbic acid (AA) can recover the intensity of the CQDs/Fe3+, indicating that CQDs/Fe3+ can be further used as a on-off fluorescent probe for the detection of AA. It is expected that our strategy may offer a new approach for developing rapid, low cost and sensitive CQDs-based sensors for environmetal and biological sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As new members in the world of nanomaterials, luminescent carbon quantum dots have gained great interest in both fundametal research and technical applications in recent years [1]. CQDs, which were first discovered serendipitously in 2004 by researchers when purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) fabricated by arc-discharge methods, have the average size below 10nm and can be dispersed in water or other organic solvents with quasi-spherical [2–4]. subsequently, many approaches to fabricate CQDs are developed, among which the most universal ones are “top-down” cutting from different carbon sources and “bottom-up” synthesis from polymers or organic molecules [5–8]. Comparing with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, which have the advantages of high photostability, large molar extinction coefficients and high photoluminescence quantum yields but suffer from low water soluble, serious cytotoxicity and environmetal hazard [9–12], CQDs have unique properties such as no-blinking, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, hydrophilicity and high stability with inhibition to photobleaching that have attracted much more attention for their promising applications in biological imaging, biological sensor, disease diagnosis, targeted tracer and drug release/delivery [13–19]. Now, CQDs have also been used as FL probes for the detection of nucleic acids, enzymes, proteins, metal ions, and other small molecules [20–23].

It is well-known that iron in its II/III ionic (Fe2+, Fe3+) states is an essential metal for heme group and plays a crucial role in a variety of vital cell functions [24]. It is critically involved in oxygen metabolism and electron transfer processes due to its facile redox chemistry and high affinity for oxygen [25,26]. However, both its deficiency and excessive from the normal permissible limit can induce serious disorders such as tissue damage, organ failure and eventually death [27]. Thus, it is vital to develop a sensitive and selective method to analysis Fe3+ ion in food, clinical, human body and different industrial products [28–30]. To date, there exists many methods for the analysis of the content of Fe3+ ion, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [31], electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) [32], mass spectroscopy [33], coupled plasma spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [34], electrochemical [35], spectrophotometric [32], However, these techniques still have many shortcomings, for example, the probes are hard to synthesis, the pre-treatment of samples are complicated and the manipulating of the instrumentations are sophisticate [36]. Thus, it is of great significant to develop a convenient, fast and simple method to determine Fe3+ ion [37,38]. Comparing with other detection methods, fluorescent chemsensors have many merits such as sensitivity, selectivity and real-time monitoring with fast response [39].

On the other hand, ascorbic acid (AA), also known as vitamin C, which is regarded as a reducing agent, has special functions in the redox interrelationship as an antioxidant and enzyme cofactor [40]. Besides, it is a vital vitamin in the diet of humans and has been used for the prevention and treatment of the common cold, metal illness, cancer and so on [41,42]. Therefore, it has received considerable attention to analyze the content of AA in foods and pharmaceutical preparations and to develop a simple and fast method for its determination is crucial. Although various methods have been employed for the quantitative determination of AA, scientists are continuing searching for better method to detect the concentration of AA [43–45].

Among various approaches of fabricating CQDs, the bottom-up methods are not so sophisticated and cost less energy. Therefore, in this article, the ionic liquid-functionalized CQDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment using citric acid and 1-aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide reacted for 2h at 240°C. Then, it can be served as a on-off fluorescent probe in the sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+. Furthermore, using the CQDs/Fe3+ mixture as an analytic platform, the FL intensity of CQDs/Fe3+ after the addition of different biomolecues were also measured, and it was found that only AA can recover the FL intensity completely which indicated that CQDs/Fe3+ can be further used as a on-off fluorescent probe to detect AA.

1-Aminopropyl-3-methyl-imidazolium bromide ([APMIm][Br]) was obtained from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (China). Citric acid monohydrate, ascorbic acid (AA) and all the metal salts used in this work including NaNO3, Mg(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, KNO3, Mn(NO3)2, Fe(NO3)3, Fe(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2, AgNO3, Cd(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2 and Ba(NO3)2 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Various neurotransmitters (norepinephrine bitartrate (NB), acetylcholine chloride (AC) or amino acids including alanine (Ala), valine (Val), phenylalanine (Phe), Tryptophan (Tyr), lysine (Lys), 5-hydroxytryptophan (5-HTP), tyrosine (Tyr), histidine (His) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. All the above reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification. The water used through all the experiments was purified through a quartz water purification system.

First, citric acid monohydrate and [APMIm][Br] with a molar ratio of 1:5 were added into a round-bottom flask which contains 20mL water. After citric acid monohydrate and [APMIm][Br] dissolved totally, water was removed by rotary evaporation and we obtained a faint yellow gel, which was then immersed in an oil bath and pyrolyzed at 240°C for 2h under argon and magnetic stirring. Then after cooling down to room temperature, added 25mL water and a reddish brown dispersion appeared, which was then dialyzed by a dialysis bag (1000Da) against water for 3days to remove the unreacted molecular precursors. Finally, the water in the dialyzate was lyophilized and the ionic-liquid modified CQDs were obtained as a brown powder, the preparing scheme is shown in Fig. 1 .

The morphologies of the products were characterized by high resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), which was performed on a JEOL JEM-2100 instrument with accelerating voltage of 200kV. The sample was prepared by placing a drop of CQDs solution on a carbon-coated copper grid and dried at room temperature. The FL emission spectra were performed on a LS-55 spectrofluorometer (PerkinElmer, Waltham, MA, USA) with a quartz cell (1×1cm). The FL lifetimes were measured on a FLS920 with a time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) method by excitation with 360nm. The datas were deconvoluted with the instrument response function, recorded using dispersed light, and fitted to a multi-exponential function.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: The selective detection of Fe3+ ion was confirmed by adding the CQDs into ultra-pure water (the final concentration was 4×10−4 mgmL−1), followed by the addition of different metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+,Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Ba2+) with a final concentration of 50mmolL−1. The sensitivity of Fe3+ ion was confirmed by adding different concentration of Fe3+ to 4×10−4 mgmL−1 CQDs solution. Besides, in order to test the practical application of the CQDs, CQDs were added into the ultra-pure water, Daming Lake, Yellow River, Black Tiger Spring, school pond and tap water, respectively. The FL spectra were recorded after reacted for 30min. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

In a typical assay, CQDs and Fe3+ ion were added into ultra-pure water (the final concentration of CQDs and Fe3+ ion were 4×10−4 mgmL−1 and 10mmolL−1, respectively), followed by the addition of various neurotransmitters and amino acids in a final concentration of 10mmolL−1. Among the above related analogues, the FL intensity of CQDs can be recovered only when adding AA. And the FL intensity after the addition of different concentrations of AA were measured. The FL spectra were recorded after reaction for 30min. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

Fig. 2 A shows the HR-TEM images of the CQDs, it can clearly be seen that the CQDs were monodisperse nanoparticles with a diameters mainly distributed in 2.5–4.5 nm(Fig. 2B). The HR-TEM images of CQDs(the inset of Fig. 2A) also showed the lattice spacing of 0.194nm, which is consistent with the (104) diffraction planes of graphitic carbons (JCPDS 26-1076) [46]. Fig. 3 shows the FL spectra of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 340nm to 440nm. The spectrums show that the intensity and peak positions of the FL emissions depended on the excitation wavelengths. The FL spectra display an emission maximum at 410nm at an excitation of 380nm. Thus, all of the FL spectra were measured at the excitation of 380nm in next experiments.

To verify the selective detection of the CQDs towards different metal ions, various metal nitrate salts, including Na+, Ag+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Al3+ and Fe3+ were added into CQDs solutions and the FL intensity changes of CQDs were measured, as shown in Fig. 4 A. It can be seen that only Fe3+ ion can quench the FL completely while other metal ions can not, and the photographs of the CQDs solution containing 10mmolL−1 various metal ions under 365nm UV light were shown in Fig. 4D. Besides, to further confirm the selectivity and anti-jamming capability of the CQDs over a variety of metal ions, different metal ions (Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Ba2+) were added into CQDs solution in the presence of Fe3+ ion and their FL intensity were measured. It can be seen from Fig. 4B that the FL intensity of CQDs were all quenched after the addition of Fe3+, indicating that other metal ions did not interfere with the results so much. For Fig. 4C, the black bars indicate the FL intensity of the CQDs in the presence of Fe3+ in the CQDs solution together with the previous individual metal ions and the red bars show the FL intensity of various metal ions without the addition of Fe3+ ion. All the results indicated that the FL intensity of the CQDs was quenched completely by Fe3+ ion but almost maintain constant with a series of other metal ions and the other metal ions show no significant impact on the quenching effect in the presence of Fe3+. These experiments obviously show that Fe3+ ion has a selective detection on our CQDs.

The sensitive detection of Fe3+ was also confirmed by adding a series amounts of Fe3+ ion into 4×10−4 mgmL−1 CQDs solution and measuring the change of FL intensity. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the FL intensity of the CQDs decrease gradually with the increasing concentration of Fe3+, indicating that Fe3+ can be quantitatively detected by the CQDs. Fig. 5B shows FL intensity changes with the concentration of Fe3+ range from 0 to 300μmolL−1 which reveals that there is a good linear correlation (R2 =0.99) between the value of (F0-F)/F0 and Fe3+ concentration. Based on 3σ/m, where σ corresponds to the standard deviation of the blank measurements and m is the slope in the plot of the intensity versus the sample concentration, the detection limit was calculated to be 13.68μmolL−1 by the FL titration assay.

Moreover, in order to demonstrate the splendid perspective of the probe in practical applications, the FL intensity of CQDs were measured in various real water of Jinan, the capital of Shandong province. As shown in Fig. 6 , among the addition of the ultra-pure water, Daming Lake, Yellow River, Black Tiger Spring, school pond and tap water, the FL intensity decrease of school pond is much higher than Daming Lake, Yellow River and tap water, indicating that the school pond contains a high concentration of Fe3+. Besides, the FL intensity decrease of Black Tiger Spring is much lower than other water which reveals that the Black Tiger Spring is of good quality and this may the reason why so many citizens in Jinan drink the water of Black Tiger Spring directly.

Since AA is so important for our health, the detection of AA in food and human body is of great significance. Therefore, we took the CQDs/Fe3+ mixture as an analytic platform to further detect the biomolecules among various neurotransmitters and amino acids (NB, AC, Ala, Val, Phe, Tyr, Lys, 5-HTP, Tyr, His). It can be observed from the FL results (Fig. 7 A) that only AA can recover the FL intensity completely while other biomolecues almost have no influence, indicating the selectivity of CQDs/Fe3+ probe toward AA. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S2A, upon the addition of different amounts of AA, the FL intensity of CQDs/Fe3+ analytical platform recovered gradually. Based on the redox mechanism between AA and Fe3+, we speculated that AA can reduce Fe3+ into Fe2+. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 7B, when adding Fe2+ and Fe3+ into CQDs, respectively, it can be observed that Fe2+ has no influence on the FL intensity of CQDs while Fe3+ has, indicating that the FL quenching of CQDs is not only due to iron element, but also the ionic state of iron. Therefore, it is demonstrated that the Fe2+ has no influence on the FL intensity of the CQDs (Fig. 7B) which made the FL intensity of CQDs recover when Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+ by AA. In order to verify the anti-interference ability of AA, in a typical assay, CQDs and Fe3+ were added into ultra-pure water, followed by the addition of various neurotransmitters and amino acids. It is clear that no significant variation was observed in the fluorescence recover caused by AA in the presence of other biomolecules (Fig. S2B). As shown in Fig. 7C, competition experiments of CQDs/Fe3+ probe showed its excellent anti-interference capability even in the presence of various interfering biomolecules. Therefore, another sensor which based on CQDs/Fe3+ analytical platform was presented to detect AA.

To get a deep insight into the quenching and recovery behavior of this system, the time-resolved FL was applied. The FL decay behavior of CQDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ion and AA were shown in Fig. 8 , and the exponential fit results are summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen that the lifetimes for pure CQDs were analysed using the double-exponential mode giving as a result two decay lifetimes of 2.2136 and 11.1042ns (χ2 =1.090). After the addition of Fe3+ ion, the lifetimes changed to be 2.3329 and 9.7729ns (χ2 =1.088). The addition of AA to CQDs/Fe3+ solution gave results of 1.9585 and 8.7132ns (χ2 =1.022), similar as pure CQDs. The changes in average lifetimes after the addition of Fe3+ ion seems to indicate that the changes in FL are due to dynamic quenching.

For the FL quenching mechanism of CQDs by metal ions, some possible reasons has been demonstrated. For example, Zhang et al. synthesized CQDs with polyoxyethylenebis (amine) (PEG-diamine) and citric acid through a high-output solid-phase thermal method and found the coordination between Fe3+ ion and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs quenched the FL intensity via nonradiative electron transfer [47]. Qu et al. synthesized CQDs from dopamine and their FL intensity were quenched by the redox reaction between catechol groups of the CQDs and Fe3+ ion [26]. Yang’s group used honey as the carbon source to synthesize CQDs and found the interactions between Fe3+ ion and COOH, OH and NH2 of CDs led to the aggregations of CQDs and quenched the FL intensity [48]. Huang’s groups developed a one-pot microwave-assisted hydrothermal method to synthesize CQDs and found the functional groups of CQDs could generate complexes with Fe3+ ion via synergistic effect, resulting in the FL quenching of CQDs [49]. Zhou’s group synthesized CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and ethylenediamine and it were quenched by the chelation between Fe3+ ion and CQDs via “N” and “O” [50].

Compared with the above FL quenching mechanism of CQDs and Fe3+ ion, the surfaces of our CQDs include lots of COOH and OH groups due to the CQDs we used were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and 1-Aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium bromide.It can be thought that the coordination between Fe3+ and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CQDs may quench the FL intensity via the nonradiative electron transfer [51], the schematic diagram is shown in Scheme 1 . Besides, the TEM image of CQDs shows that the diameter of CQDs changed a lot in the presence and absence of Fe3+,the pure CQDs can hardly be seen in the TEM results in Fig. 9 , while nanoparticles with average diameter of 80nm can be clearly seen after the addition of Fe3+. This indicates that there had an aggregation of CQDs after adding Fe3+ into the CQDs solution and the FL intensity was quenched. Then, various neurotransmitters and amino acids were added into the CQDS/Fe3+ platform and only AA can recover the FL intensity of CQDs. Since AA can redox Fe3+ to Fe2+ ion and Fe2+ has no influences on the FL intensity of CQDs, thus, it can be speculated that the FL recovery is due to the redox mechanism.

In summary, a facile, convenient and straightforward hydrothermal method for the fabrication of CQDs were developed with strong FL quenching response to Fe3+ ion, while other metal ions have no influence on the FL quenching of CQDs. Moreover, based on the redox mechanism, after the addition of AA, the FL intensity recovered obviously while other biomolecules can not. All our results indicated that our CQDs can act as a chemsensor for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+ and then the CQDs/Fe3+ platform can further serve as a probe for the detection of AA against other analogue such as various neurotransmitters and amino acids. It is believed that our CQDs may offer a new approach for developing a convenient, fast, sensitive and selective sensor in environmetal applications and biological sensing applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21203109), and Ji’nan Youth Science and Technology Star Program (2013040).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2017.05.069.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Carbon dots: synthetic methods and applications as fluorescent probes for the detection of metal ions, inorganic anions and organic molecules
A general quantitative pH sensor developed with dicyandiamide N-doped high quantum yield graphene quantum dots
Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection
The photoluminescence mechanism in carbon dots (graphene quantum dots, carbon nanodots, and polymer dots): current state and future perspective
Synthesis and modification of carbon nanomaterials utilizing microwave heating
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Toward biocompatible semiconductor quantum dots: from biosynthesis and bioconjugation to biomedical application
Quantum dots versus organic dyes as fluorescent labels
Quantum-size-controlled photoelectrochemical fabrication of epitaxial InGaN quantum dots
Highly selective detection of phosphate in very complicated matrixes with an off-on fluorescent probe of europium-adjusted carbon dots
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapidsensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Development of a carbon quantum dots-based fluorescent Cu2+ probe suitable for living cell imaging
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Carbon-dot-based dual-emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
Carbon dots based dual-emission silica nanoparticles as a ratiometric nanosensor for Cu2+
Facile synthesis of highly luminescent co-doped carbon nanodots for rapidsensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+
Efficient fluorescence quenching in carbon dots by surface-doped metals-disruption of excited state redox processes and mechanistic implications
A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature
Toward biocompatible semiconductor quantum dots: from biosynthesis and bioconjugation to biomedical application

An efficient and selective flourescent chemical sensor based on 5-(8-hydroxy-2-quinolinylmethyl)-2,8-dithia-5-aza-2,6-pyridinophane as a new fluoroionophore for determination of iron(III) ions. A novel probe for iron speciation
Response of pore water AlFe and S concentrations to waterlogging in a boreal acid sulphate soil
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron(III) ions and dopamine
Facile preparation of fluorescent polydihydroxyphenylalanine nanoparticles for label-free detection of copper ions
Colorimetric detection of Fe(III) ions using label-free gold nanoparticles and acidic thiourea mixture
Fluorescent oligomer as a chemosensor for the label-free detection of Fe3+ and dopamine with selectivity and sensitivity
Determination of iron species in wine by ion-exchange chromatography-flame atomic absorption spectrometry
Intracellular free iron in liver tissue and liver homogenate: studies with electron paramagnetic resonance on the formation of paramagnetic complexes with desferal and nitric oxide
Determination of iron in seawater by high-resolution isotope dilution inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry after Mg(OH)2 coprecipitation
Detection of iron species using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry under cold plasma temperature conditions
Application of a bismuth film electrode to the voltammetric determination of trace iron using a Fe(III)-TEA-BrO3-catalytic system
On-off-on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a turn-off fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Tunable amphiphilicity and multifunctional applications of ionic-liquid-modified carbon quantum dots
Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for off-on sensing of Hg(II) and I−
Ascorbic acid supplementation improves skeletal muscle oxidative stress and insulin sensitivity in people with type 2 diabetes: findings of a randomized controlled study
Ascorbic acid and colon cancer: an oxidative stimulus to cell death depending on cell profile
A novel biological role of dehydroascorbic acid: inhibition of Na+-dependent transport of ascorbic acid
A sensitive and selective chemosensor for ascorbic acid based on a fluorescent nitroxide switch
Copper nanoclusters as an on-off-on fluorescent probe for ascorbic acid
Simultaneous detection of dopamine and ascorbic acid using silicate network interlinked gold nanoparticles and multi-walled carbon nanotubes
Nanosized carbon particles from natural gas soot
Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Novel and green synthesis of high-fluorescent carbon dots originated from honey for sensing and imaging
Histidine-derived nontoxic nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging applications
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots by doping nitrogen element and its application in the detection of Fe(III)
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles for the fluorescent detection of metal ions
